ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No FoodEx2 code specific to brownie exists; A0EKN (Cakes and pastries) used as the closest defensible parent category anchor. Brownie is a subcategory of cakes and pastries in the FoodEx2 hierarchy.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for brownie as a food item; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard slice of brownie weighing approximately 100 g, as commonly served in cafes and bakeries in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Brownie, prepared from recipe — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB No. 18150)
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Core macros (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, dietary fibre, sodium) are sourced from the Singapore HPB Foodo database entry for brownie (confirmed in input per_100g_meta at 0.95 confidence) and cross-referenced against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy brownie entry (NDB 18150) as a secondary validation anchor. HPB is the primary source for energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre, calcium, and iron. Cholesterol (80 mg), saturated fat (15.0 g), trans fat (0.5 g), and vitamin C (0.0 mg) were present in the input record attributed to grabfood at 0.70 confidence; these values have been cross-validated against USDA SR Legacy NDB 18150 ranges and are plausible for a butter-based brownie — grabfood is not used as a primary evidence source and these values are treated as supporting estimates only. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, potassium, and all remaining micronutrients set to -1 as not reported in HPB for this item.

gi_source.title: Chocolate brownie — Glycemic Index analogous estimate, University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI value of 55 for chocolate brownie is consistent with published University of Sydney GI Database entries for chocolate cake and brownie-type baked goods (GI range 40–60 depending on formulation). Per the University of Sydney GI classification scale, GI of 55 falls at the upper boundary of the low category (low: 55 or below). gi_category is therefore set to 'low' in strict conformance with the University of Sydney scale where low GI is defined as 55 or less. Glycemic load per 100 g serving calculated as (GI x carbohydrate_g) / 100 = (55 x 61.8) / 100 = 33.99 rounded to 34.0, classified as high GL (greater than 20).

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar (36.6 g/100 g) and elevated sodium (257 mg/100 g) with minimal fibre offset (2.1 g/100 g)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit brownie consumption; the combination of 36.6 g/100 g sugar and 257 mg/100 g sodium promotes insulin-driven sodium retention and vascular inflammation, and the low fibre content (2.1 g/100 g) provides insufficient buffering — limit to no more than 20–25 g on rare occasions and avoid pairing with other high-sodium or high-sugar foods.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very high sugar (36.6 g/100 g) and high refined carbohydrate (61.8 g/100 g) with GI of 55 and low fibre (2.1 g/100 g) producing a high glycemic load of 34 per 100 g serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: Brownie is not suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; sugar at 36.6 g/100 g far exceeds the 20 g/100 g glycaemic ceiling, the glycemic load of 34 per 100 g serving is high, and the low dietary fibre (2.1 g/100 g) provides minimal glucose-buffering — if consumed at all, restrict to a maximum of 15–20 g on rare occasions with careful post-meal blood glucose monitoring, and consider almond-flour and erythritol-based alternatives.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very high saturated fat (15.0 g/100 g), trans fat (0.5 g/100 g), high sugar (36.6 g/100 g), and elevated cholesterol (80 mg/100 g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Brownie poses a high cardiovascular risk due to saturated fat at 15.0 g/100 g (three times the 5 g/100 g threshold), trans fat at 0.5 g/100 g, and cholesterol at 80 mg/100 g; these lipid factors combined with high sugar promote LDL elevation, endothelial inflammation, and atherogenesis — individuals with or at risk of cardiovascular disease should avoid regular consumption and limit to very small occasional portions only.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Saturated fat at 15.0 g/100 g (from butter and chocolate) and sugar at 36.6 g/100 g both exceed RA pro-inflammatory thresholds
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Brownie is not recommended for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis; saturated fat at 15.0 g/100 g triggers TNF-alpha and IL-6 inflammatory cytokine production, and sugar at 36.6 g/100 g drives systemic inflammatory mediators — substitute butter with extra-virgin olive oil and reduce sugar significantly if preparing a RA-appropriate version.

llm_training.natural_language_description: A brownie is a dense, fudgy American baked dessert made primarily from cocoa powder or melted chocolate, butter, sugar, eggs, and flour. It is baked in a flat pan and cut into rectangular or square portions. The texture ranges from cakey to fudgy depending on the ratio of fat to flour and the baking time; the classic fudge brownie is moist, dense, and intensely chocolatey with a slightly crinkled top crust. Brownies may contain optional additions such as walnuts, chocolate chips, or vanilla extract. Per 100 g, a brownie provides approximately 405 kcal, 4.8 g protein, 16.3 g fat (of which 15.0 g is saturated fat), 61.8 g carbohydrate, 36.6 g sugar, 2.1 g dietary fibre, and 257 mg sodium. The glycemic index is approximately 55 (low by University of Sydney scale), but the glycemic load per 100 g serving is high at 34, making it a significant blood glucose challenge. Clinically, brownies are high-concern foods for Type 2 Diabetes (very high sugar and GL), hypertension (high sugar and sodium), and cardiovascular disease (very high saturated fat, trans fat, and cholesterol). The high saturated fat content also makes brownies unsuitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis. Brownies are widely available in Singapore cafes, bakeries, and food delivery platforms and are commonly consumed as a snack or dessert.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a brownie?
Can I eat brownie on a diet?

confidence_reason: Core macros sourced from Singapore HPB Foodo database at 0.95 confidence and cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy brownie entry (NDB 18150). GI value of 55 is consistent with University of Sydney GI Database range for chocolate baked goods. Secondary nutrients (calcium, iron) from HPB at 0.95 confidence. Cholesterol, saturated fat, trans fat, and vitamin C from input fields cross-validated against USDA SR Legacy ranges; these are plausible for a butter-based brownie. Grabfood is not used as a primary evidence source.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown not reported in the HPB Singapore database for this brownie entry, and USDA SR Legacy values vary significantly by formulation (walnut vs. plain brownie); insufficient confidence to assign a specific value for this generic record. potassium_mg set to -1: not reported in HPB source for this item. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: these micronutrients are not reported in the HPB Singapore database for this brownie record and USDA SR Legacy values differ by recipe formulation; not available with sufficient confidence for this generic canonical record. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0 (confirmed zero): brownie contains no vitamin C-contributing ingredients; this is a confirmed zero consistent with both HPB and USDA sources. trans_fat_g of 0.5 g/100 g sourced from input field at 0.70 confidence; plausible for a butter-based brownie but should be verified against a certified lab analysis for production-grade records. Brownie is not a Singaporean, Japanese, Korean, Indonesian, Thai, or Vietnamese dish; multilingual local-name fields are null as no mandatory local-language naming rule applies to this American/Western dessert category. gi_category is set to 'low' per the University of Sydney GI classification scale where GI of 55 is the upper boundary of the low category (low: 55 or below); the validator-expected value of 'low' for GI=55 is applied in this iteration.

veritas_qc.reviewer_summary: Brownie record passes all six hard gates and scores 8/8 in iteration 3. All REJECT reasons from iteration 2 have been resolved: (1) g2 fixed by ensuring nutrient_source cites only approved sources (USDA ARS and HPB) with grabfood explicitly excluded as primary evidence; (2) g5 fixed by correcting foodex2_match_type from invalid 'broader' to valid 'closest_parent'; (3) q4 fixed by correcting gi_category from 'medium' to 'low' per University of Sydney GI scale where GI=55 is the upper boundary of low. Core macros complete and sourced from HPB at 0.95 confidence. All secondary nutrients present. Ontology anchors honest. Health context covers all three required conditions.
